chat_messages.text: hi! how do I use rg to search all text contained in files within directory? (mac bash)

content.text: hi! how do I use rg to search all text contained in files within directory? (mac bash)

chat_messages.text:
You can use `rg` (ripgrep) to search text within files in a directory like this:

```bash
# Search for "pattern" in current directory and subdirectories
rg "your_search_term"

# Search in a specific directory
rg "your_search_term" /path/to/directory

# Search with case-insensitive matching
rg -i "your_search_term"

# Show line numbers
rg -n "your_search_term"

# Search only specific file types
rg -t py "your_search_term"  # only Python files
rg -t js "your_search_term"  # only JavaScript files

# Search and show context (lines before/after matches)
rg -C 3 "your_search_term"   # 3 lines before and after
rg -A 2 -B 1 "your_search_term"  # 2 after, 1 before
```

By default, `rg` recursively searches all files in the current directory and ignores binary files, hidden files, and files in `.gitignore`. It's much faster than `grep -r` and has sensible defaults for most use cases.

If you don't have ripgrep installed yet, you can install it with: `brew install ripgrep`

You can use `rg` (ripgrep) to search text within files in a directory like this:

```bash
# Search for "pattern" in current directory and subdirectories
rg "your_search_term"

# Search in a specific directory
rg "your_search_term" /path/to/directory

# Search with case-insensitive matching
rg -i "your_search_term"

rg -C 3 "your_search_term"   # 3 lines before and after
rg -A 2 -B 1 "your_search_term"  # 2 after, 1 before
```

By default, `rg` recursively searches all files in the current directory and ignores binary files, hidden files, and files in `.gitignore`. It's much faster than `grep -r` and has sensible defaults for most use cases.

If you don't have ripgrep installed yet, you can install it with: `brew install ripgrep`

chat_messages.text: now, how do I also print the filename that results are in?

content.text: now, how do I also print the filename that results are in?

chat_messages.text:
`rg` prints filenames by default when there are matches in multiple files! But here are some options to control filename display:

```bash
# Default behavior - shows filenames when searching multiple files
rg "your_search_term"

# Force filename display even for single files
rg -H "your_search_term"

# Show only filenames (no match content)
rg -l "your_search_term"

# Suppress filename display (content only)
rg -h "your_search_term"

# Show filename with line numbers
rg -n "your_search_term"
```

The output normally looks like:
```
src/main.py:15:    print("your_search_term found here")
docs/readme.md:3:# This contains your_search_term
```

If you're only getting content without filenames, you might be searching a single file or piping input. Use `-H` to force filename display in those cases.

content.text:
`rg` prints filenames by default when there are matches in multiple files! But here are some options to control filename display:

```bash
# Default behavior - shows filenames when searching multiple files
rg "your_search_term"

# Force filename display even for single files
rg -H "your_search_term"

# Show only filenames (no match content)
rg -l "your_search_term"

# Suppress filename display (content only)
rg -h "your_search_term"

# Show filename with line numbers
rg -n "your_search_term"
```

The output normally looks like:
```
src/main.py:15:    print("your_search_term found here")
docs/readme.md:3:# This contains your_search_term
```

If you're only getting content without filenames, you might be searching a single file or piping input. Use `-H` to force filename display in those cases.
